blocks.body: Some and any help talk about an indefinite number of people or things. They work with uncountable nouns and plurals.

• Some - usually in statements: We have some bread.
  • Any — in questions and objections: Do you have any milk?
  • Both are used with uncountables and plurals: some apples, any furniture.
  • We do not put countable nouns in the singular: we use a/an.
blocks.heading: Basic Rule

blocks.body:
• In questions with an offer/request: Would you like some tea?
  • In questions when we expect a "yes" answer: Did you find some tickets?
  • In expressions with someone, somebody, somewhere - any statements.
Could I have some water?
Can I have some water?
blocks.heading: Exceptions for some

blocks.body:
• In statements with the tone "any": You can take any seat.
  • After hardly, without and the like - conveys a negation: She left without any luggage.
  • In regular expressions: any time, any day.
blocks.heading: Exceptions for any

blocks.body:
Form Value Example
someone / somebody Someone Someone is waiting for you.
anyone / anybody Anybody Anybody can join.
somewhere Somewhere Let's go somewhere warm.
anywhere Anywhere, Nowhere We can meet anywhere.
something / anything Something / anything Is there anything to eat?
blocks.heading: Related words

blocks.body:
🧠

For uncountable nouns, use some/any and additional words of quantity: a piece of advice, a bit of luck.

To make the objection softer, use not any → We don’t have any sugar.
blocks.heading: Tips
